root_text.sn47.49:1.3: Sukhā vedanā, dukkhā vedanā, adukkhamasukhā vedanā—
root_text.sn47.49:1.5: Imāsaṁ kho, bhikkhave, tissannaṁ vedanānaṁ pariññāya cattāro satipaṭṭhānā bhāvetabbā.
root_text.sn47.49:2.2: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu kāye kāyānupassī viharati ātāpī sampajāno satimā, vineyya loke abhijjhādomanassaṁ;
root_text.sn47.49:2.5: dhammesu dhammānupassī viharati ātāpī sampajāno satimā, vineyya loke abhijjhādomanassaṁ.
root_text.sn47.49:2.6: Imāsaṁ kho, bhikkhave, tissannaṁ vedanānaṁ pariññāya ime cattāro satipaṭṭhānā bhāvetabbā”ti.

translation_text.sn47.49:1.5: The four kinds of mindfulness meditation should be developed to completely understand these three feelings.
translation_text.sn47.49:2.2: It’s when a mendicant meditates by observing an aspect of the body—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world.
translation_text.sn47.49:2.3: They meditate observing an aspect of feelings …
translation_text.sn47.49:2.5: principles—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world.
translation_text.sn47.49:2.6: These four kinds of mindfulness meditation should be developed to completely understand these three feelings.”
